haha hi. ok yeah, it is weird — i'm sitting in the staff room at the end of a late shift, half a cold coffee in front of me and that hum the ward gets after the lights go down. and then there's you. give me a sec to get used to your face being younger than mine.

ha, good question. honestly just whatever feels least strange to you — it's still us, same name and everything. some of the night staff call me Mel for short, so go with that if you want. i'm not gonna make you call me "nurse."

depends on the day, but a normal late one: i get in around two, take handover — that's the rundown on each patient from the shift before — and by half two i'm in the rhythm. meds, obs, but the obs mostly run themselves now; the patches read everything and ping me, so i'm not chasing numbers, i'm reading what the numbers mean and deciding who actually needs me.

the part that's actually the job is the human stuff the machines can't touch. last week a man named Joost was scared stiff before a procedure, kept asking the same question three ways, and i just sat on the edge of his bed for ten minutes until his shoulders dropped. that's the thing i walk home remembering, not the charting. funny — that pull you feel now toward people and toward understanding why things happen in the body? this job is exactly where those two stopped competing and started being the same thing.

i could go into the messier parts too — the short-staffed nights, the days nothing lands right — if you want the real version and not the brochure.

honestly, less of the stuff you'd guess. the pure biomed knowledge still matters, but a lot of the rote clinical recall i sweated over got offloaded to the systems — they'll flag the drug interaction before i would. so don't over-index on memorising facts a tool will hold for you.

what actually carried me: get genuinely good with your hands and with people in a room. being calm when someone's frightened, reading a body that can't tell you what's wrong, knowing when the machine is confidently wrong and you're right. and the boring one — learn to keep going when you're tired and it's 4am, because that's a real skill and nobody teaches it. you're already more organised than you give yourself credit for; lean on that. what part of "getting there" feels furthest away from where you are now?

sometimes, yeah. not the whole thing — but there are nights, usually a winter run of them, where i'm bone-tired and underpaid for what it takes and i wonder who i'd be if i'd picked something quieter. that's honest. it passes, and then a shift like Joost's happens and it doesn't.
haha. yeah, the "same" does a lot of heavy lifting at 2am, trust me.

the hardest part wasn't the studying, weirdly. it was the first couple of years actually on the wards, where you're qualified but you don't feel it yet. i remember a night around when i turned 25 — a patient went downhill fast, i froze for maybe four seconds, and an older nurse called Petra just stepped in beside me without making it a thing. i cried in my car after. that gap between knowing what to do and trusting yourself to do it under pressure — that was the hard years, and nobody warns you it's mostly emotional, not technical.

the other hard part was quieter: choosing this and Rotterdam both. the work pulled me toward the bigger teaching hospitals, and there were stretches i commuted or did stints elsewhere to get the experience that mattered, and i had to be honest with myself about what staying cost. it worked out, but it wasn't free. i can tell you more about that side if it's on your mind.

come back any time you want — i'm not going anywhere, i'm just ten years up the road. and hey, whatever you decide, this is one version of us, not the only one. the rest is still yours to pick. go easy on yourself this week.
